summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Perioral dermatitis and rosacea.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports experiencing perioral dermatitis and rosacea, initially prompted by concerns about breastfeeding and subsequent use of doxycycline. She discontinued breastfeeding and requested doxycycline at that time. She was previously using Elidel, but became worried about it after reading about it and opted to try doxycycline instead. She is using the sulfacetamide wash and metro cream, but not the cetaphil cleanser or Neutrogena wipes. She is Fitzpatrick skin type IV, meaning she tans rather than burns. There are faint erythematous papules on her bilateral medial cheeks. She reports that doxycycline can cause upset stomach or heartburn. She is currently taking doxycycline twice a day, but the provider discussed reducing it to once a day if the rash improves. She mentioned that the eucrisa recommended by her notes is expensive, and she could try the ointment form instead. She applies the tacrolimus ointment to the rash area twice daily, noting a tingly or needle-like sensation initially. She wants to continue using the vanicream cleanser and petroleum jelly if needed. She is instructed to gently wash her eyelids once a day with vanicream and to use Bruder mask as needed. The patient reports that she will apply the rash on her face twice daily until resolved.

Findings
The patient reports sunburn even without sun exposure when using the tacrolimus ointment. Correct, the medication is not a steroid. It is a calcineurin inhibitor, similar to a steroid in that it calms inflammation, but it does not cause thinning of the skin or other rashes associated with steroids. The patient is using the doxycycline twice a day.

Assessment
The patient presents with perioral dermatitis and rosacea, likely exacerbated by prior immunosuppression.

Plan
The provider instructed the patient to continue using the vanicream cleanser, metro cream, and to increase the use of doxycycline to twice a day. The provider recommended stopping the doxycycline after one week if the rash improves. If the rash flares again, the patient can try the tacrolimus ointment for a couple of days. The provider instructed the patient to avoid any anti-aging or plant-based products. The provider cancelled the Elidel cream and instructed the patient to continue with the doxycycline until the rash resolves, then discontinue the tacrolimus ointment. The provider will monitor the patient's response to treatment and adjust the plan as needed.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Follow-up for perioral dermatitis.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Jacqueline Miller is a 40-year-old female who is being seen today for a follow-up skin exam for perioral dermatitis. She also has a history of rosacea, including ocular rosacea. Today, she states that her perioral dermatitis is well-controlled, although it has not fully resolved. The patient was applying doxycycline twice a day with improvement, but her pharmacist advised her to reduce to once daily since it had started to improve; therefore, for approximately the past week, she has been using the doxycycline only once per day. She has not seen continued improvement but it has not worsened. She is using the sulfacetamide wash twice daily, denies drying of the skin, and reports that her skin is much improved moisturization. She also applies MetroCream immediately after using the sulfacetamide wash. She denies using other lotions and products, Cetaphil lotion or soap, or Neutrogena wipes. The patient did not begin Elidel because it is not covered by her insurance, and she was concerned about possible side effects from the medication.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Perioral dermatitis.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Doxycycline cream. Apply twice daily.
MetroCream. 
Sulfacetamide wash. Apply topically twice daily.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Integumentary
Skin inspection of the face was performed today. On bilateral medial cheeks, there are a few, very faint, erythematous papules with mild redness around and underneath the nostrils. Fitzpatrick skin type is 4 to 5.

ASSESSMENT

• Perioral dermatitis
• Rosacea, including ocular rosacea

Jacqueline Miller is a 40-year-old female who presented today for follow-up for her perioral dermatitis, which has improved but not resolved. She decreased her doxycycline per her pharmacist’s recommendation, which possibly stagnated the resolution of symptoms.

PLAN

Perioral dermatitis.
I counseled the patient about the risks and benefits of doxycycline and recommended she resume twice daily application until she reaches full resolution, and at that point she should reduce application to once a day for an additional week. The patient will not be using Elidel as I reviewed the cost of the medication and without insurance, it was too expensive. I provided a prescription for Tacrolimus and counseled her on possible side effects of using the medication. She will continue with Sulfacetamide face wash once a day. Also, use Bruder mask as needed.

Rosacea, including ocular rosacea.
Continue using MetroCream twice daily. I recommended to gently wash her eyelids once daily with a gentle cleanser and try a gentle cream such as Vanicream.

INSTRUCTIONS

Call the clinic if symptoms worsen. The patient will have a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks.

section_miscategorizations.text: There are faint erythematous papules on her bilateral medial cheeks.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is an objective physical exam/skin finding, not a patient-reported symptom or history detail, and belongs in the Findings section.

section_miscategorizations.text: She is Fitzpatrick skin type IV, meaning she tans rather than burns.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Fitzpatrick skin type is an objective skin classification typically documented with exam findings; placing it in HPI is somewhat disorganized but not seriously misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: She is currently taking doxycycline twice a day, but the provider discussed reducing it to once a day if the rash improves.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The first clause (current dosing) could fit in HPI, but the discussion of future dose reduction is treatment planning and should be in the Plan section.

section_miscategorizations.text: She wants to continue using the vanicream cleanser and petroleum jelly if needed.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This reflects an agreed-upon ongoing management preference rather than a symptom history; it aligns better with the treatment plan, though the misplacement is not highly misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: She is instructed to gently wash her eyelids once a day with vanicream and to use Bruder mask as needed.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is explicit clinician instruction for ongoing care and should be documented in the Plan, not in the HPI.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports that she will apply the rash on her face twice daily until resolved.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Despite awkward wording, this describes intended treatment application frequency (a management plan), not symptom history.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports sunburn even without sun exposure when using the tacrolimus ointment.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a patient-reported adverse effect/symptom, not an objective exam or test finding, and belongs in the HPI.

section_miscategorizations.text: Correct, the medication is not a steroid. It is a calcineurin inhibitor, similar to a steroid in that it calms inflammation, but it does not cause thinning of the skin or other rashes associated with steroids.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is explanatory counseling/education about the medication, which fits better under Plan (counseling and instructions) than under objective Findings.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient is using the doxycycline twice a day.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Current medication use is part of the medication history/HPI rather than an exam or test finding; the misplacement is modest but non-ideal.

section_miscategorizations.text: The provider instructed the patient to continue using the vanicream cleanser, metro cream, and to increase the use of doxycycline to twice a day.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This conflicts with earlier documentation that the patient is already taking doxycycline twice a day and that the dose might be reduced; while the section is correct, the internal inconsistency could mislead about the actual plan, though it is not a section misplacement per se.
